✦ Pune, India · Class 10 · 2026

Hi, I'm
Sourish.

I'm a Class 10 student from Pune who spends too much time staring at the sky from my balcony. I observe with a 130mm Newtonian Reflector — under city light pollution, which makes things harder but more satisfying when something actually shows up. I also write small Python tools to help myself understand the physics and astronomy I'm learning.

View My Projects See My Observations
18°31′N 73°51′E
Pune · Maharashtra

// 01 · About

About Me

I got into astronomy the way a lot of people do — I looked up one night and got curious. That curiosity turned into buying a telescope, which turned into late evenings on the balcony trying to find things through light-polluted Pune skies. It's not ideal for stargazing, but it's what I have, and I've learned to work with it.

I'm in Class 10 right now, which means most of my time goes to school. But the time left over usually goes toward either setting up the telescope, reading about something I don't fully understand yet, or writing Python code to make sense of a concept I've been working through. I learn through a mix of observation, books, online courses, and just experimenting — sometimes all four at once.

Python became useful to me because it lets me actually test ideas. Instead of just reading about how moon illumination changes over a lunar cycle, I tracked it myself and wrote a program to graph it. That kind of hands-on approach helps things stick.

This website is a record of what I'm building and learning. It's not a polished showcase — it's more like a notebook that happens to be online.

Interests
AstronomyPhysics PythonAstrophotography Scientific Computing

Location
Pune, India
Currently
Board exam prep + evening observations

↑ View from my balcony · Nov 2025

// 02 · Code & Physics

Projects

01 · Python · Logic & Simulation

Rocket Staging Logic

Rockets shed their empty fuel tanks mid-flight — a technique called staging — so they don't carry dead weight all the way to orbit. I wanted to understand why this matters and how much difference it actually makes, so I wrote a Python program that models it.

The program takes inputs like payload mass, fuel mass, and the number of stages, then calculates how those stages affect the overall mass ratio and estimated velocity change (delta-v). It's a simplified model — it doesn't account for atmospheric drag or variable thrust — but it gave me a much clearer picture of why multi-stage rockets exist. The Tsiolkovsky rocket equation started making a lot more sense once I could run numbers through it myself.

Tools
Python
Concepts
Rocket stagingDelta-vMass ratio
View Code on GitHub →
02 · Python · Matplotlib · Data

Lunar Illumination Plotter

During a waning phase, I spent 16 evenings recording how much of the Moon appeared illuminated — just me, the telescope, and a spreadsheet. The numbers dropped from around 90% down to near zero as the Moon moved through its cycle.

I stored the data in a CSV file and wrote a Python script using Matplotlib to turn those numbers into a graph. The program reads the file, parses each date and illumination value, and plots how the illumination changed over time. My first attempt crashed because I had "Day 1" written as text instead of the number 1 — a small bug that took longer than I'd like to admit to find. Seeing the smooth decay curve finally appear on screen after fixing it was genuinely satisfying. It taught me that working with real observational data is messier than textbook examples, and that visualizing data makes patterns obvious that aren't obvious in a table of numbers.

Tools
PythonMatplotlibCSV
Concepts
Data visualizationLunar phasesObservational data
View Code on GitHub →
03 · Python · Physics

Kinematics Calculator

A Python tool built while learning one-dimensional motion. The calculator uses standard kinematic equations to solve problems involving velocity, acceleration, displacement, and time. You enter the values you know, and it works out the ones you don't.

Building it helped me understand the relationships between the formulas rather than just memorising them. When you have to write the logic yourself — deciding which equation to use based on which variables are provided — you start to see why the equations are structured the way they are. It's also just useful for checking answers.

Tools
Python
Concepts
Kinematics1D motionPhysics equations

// 03 · Observations

Telescope Observations

⟐ 130mm Newtonian Reflector  ·  ◎ Pune, India  ·  ☁ Light-polluted skies

Most of my observations are made from my apartment balcony in Pune. City light pollution limits what's visible, but planets and the Moon hold up well even under bad skies. The photographs below were taken by holding a smartphone against the eyepiece — a technique called afocal photography. They're not clean images, but they're real ones.

☽ Moon · May 2026
Waning Gibbous Moon — Terminator Detail
May 2026

Focused on the terminator region — the line separating the lit and unlit halves. During this phase the shadows are long, which makes craters much easier to see than during a full moon when everything is washed out by flat lighting.

The smartphone struggled with exposure. The Moon is very bright against a dark field and the camera kept trying to compensate. Had to take many shots to get one that wasn't overblown.

Observation note: Best lunar detail is always near the terminator, not at full moon.
☽ Moon · Crater Region
Lunar Surface Close-up
2026

Tried to get a closer look at the crater fields. The circular vignette in the image is the telescope eyepiece barrel — it's unavoidable with afocal smartphone photography.

The dark patches are mare regions — ancient lava plains that filled large impact basins billions of years ago. They look smooth from Earth but are actually covered with smaller craters when you zoom in further.

Observation note: The blue fringe on the edge is chromatic aberration — a phone camera artefact at high contrast boundaries.
♃ Jupiter + Moons · Feb 2026
Jupiter & Galilean Moons
February 2026

Three of the Galilean moons are visible as small dots flanking Jupiter's disc. Their arrangement changes every night as they orbit, so I cross-referenced with Stellarium after the session to work out which moons I was actually looking at.

Even a 130mm reflector shows them clearly. Galileo observed the same four moons with a far smaller instrument in 1610, which is something I kept thinking about during this session.

Observation note: Seeing conditions affect planetary detail significantly. Atmospheric turbulence above the city is the main limiting factor, not the telescope aperture.
♃ Jupiter Disc · Feb 2026
Jupiter — Cloud Bands
February 2026

Attempted to capture Jupiter's cloud bands. This took many attempts — the phone camera autofocus kept hunting. Faint colour differences in the bands are just about visible in the image.

At this magnification with a 130mm aperture, fine detail is right at the edge of what's achievable, especially from a city balcony. A dedicated astronomy camera and more aperture would make a significant difference.

Observation note: Getting colour detail from a smartphone through a small reflector is genuinely difficult. Most sessions are purely visual.
★ Star Field · Jan 2026
Night Sky from Pune
January 2026

A wide-field shot taken from the rooftop without the telescope — just a phone camera pointed at the sky. Several hundred stars are visible despite Pune's light pollution.

The brighter point near the lower-right is a planet. I haven't confirmed which one. Compared to a rural dark site, the sky background here is noticeably brighter, which wipes out fainter objects. Most deep-sky objects aren't realistic targets from where I observe.

Observation note: Planets and the Moon are largely unaffected by light pollution. Most other things are not.

// 07 · Learning

Certifications

The courses below reflect topics I've explored outside of school — mainly astronomy, space science, and scientific learning. Most of what I learn comes from a combination of books, online courses, telescope observations, and personal projects. These certificates represent some of the structured learning I've completed.

// 08 · Notes & Learnings

Recent Notes

Learning one question at a time.
Debugging My First Python Graph
Turns out my program crashed because my spreadsheet contained "DAY 1" instead of the number 1. Fixing that bug and finally seeing the graph appear was surprisingly satisfying.
→
Balancing Board Exams and Astronomy
Finding time for astronomy while preparing for board exams can be difficult. Most observing sessions currently happen in short windows during the evening.
→
Working Through Newton's Laws
Free-body diagrams are starting to make more sense. One of my next goals is writing Python scripts that visualize forces and motion — making the abstract concrete.
→

// 10 · Life

Beyond Astronomy

Astronomy and programming are some of my biggest interests, but they aren't the only things I spend time on. Through public speaking, Model United Nations, trekking, school activities, and community support, I've had opportunities to learn skills that go beyond the classroom.

PPSAMUN 6th Edition · Receiving award
Committee session · Speaking
Diplomacy & Research
Model United Nations
I participate in MUN conferences where I research international issues, prepare position papers, and take part in committee discussions. MUN has helped me improve my research, critical thinking, and public speaking while learning about global challenges and diplomacy.
School public speaking event · 2026
Speaking & Communication
Debates & Presentations
I enjoy taking part in debates, presentations, and public speaking opportunities at school. These experiences have helped me become more confident when communicating ideas and explaining complex topics to different audiences.
Hilltop viewpoint · Dec 2025
Outdoors & Nature
Trekking & Exploration
Trekking gives me the opportunity to explore nature, experience new environments, and spend time outdoors. Whether it's a short hike or a longer trek, I enjoy observing landscapes, wildlife, and the night sky away from city lights.
Inclusion & Care
Community Support
I have provided writing support to a student who required assistance during academic activities and examinations. Through this experience, I learned the importance of responsibility, patience, and helping others access educational opportunities effectively.
ResponsibilityPatienceCommunicationTeamwork
Certificate of Participation · Rijnlands Lyceum Oegstgeest, Netherlands · May 2025
Culture & Global Exchange
EUMIND Student Exchange Programme
I participated in the EUMIND (Europe Meets India) Student Exchange Programme, which gave me the opportunity to interact with students from the Netherlands and experience a different culture, education system, and way of life.
The exchange involved collaborative activities, presentations, cultural experiences, and discussions that helped me develop communication, teamwork, and cross-cultural understanding.
This experience broadened my perspective and encouraged me to think beyond my immediate environment while learning from people with different backgrounds and ideas.
Cross-cultural communicationCollaborationAdaptabilityPublic speakingGlobal awareness
